items.title: Nokia vs Asus and Acer: Regional Court Munich redefines FRAND rules
items.title_zh: 诺基亚诉华硕宏碁：慕尼黑地方法院重塑 FRAND 规则
items.summary: Munich Regional Court ordered Asus and Acer to cease and desist in an infringement case concerning a Nokia video coding patent.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles EIP Prüfer and Partner mbB PAUSTIAN & PARTNER View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Dr. Dieter Kamlah Alex Wilson Dr. Marco Stief, LL.M. View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Last Thursday, Munich Regional Court ordered Asus and Acer to cease and desist in an infringement case concerning a Nokia video coding patent. The court delivered its judgment on the same day as the hearing. The court indicated further modifications to the Munich FRAND rules.

Just yesterday, the Federal Court of Justice upheld an injunction by the Regional and Higher Regional Court of Munich against HMD, largely confirming current Munich FRAND case law. Only days earlier, the Munich Regional Court heard another FRAND case. The dispute between Nokia and computer manufacturers Asus and Acer concerned the infringement of Nokia’s EP 2 375 749 for video coding.

The 7th Civil Chamber at Munich Regional Court, under presiding judge Oliver Schön, heard the two lawsuits for a full day on 22 January. On the same day the judges found that Acer and Asus are not willing to take a licence and had infringed EP 749 (case IDs: Acer 7 O 4100/25 and Asus 7 O 4102/25). It is considered certain that Nokia will enforce the judgments and that both implementers will appeal.

In spring 2025, Nokia filed patent claims against Acer, Asus and Hisense at Munich Regional Court and the UPC over video codec patents. Nokia alleged that Acer and Asus’ PCs and Hisense’s smart TVs infringe three of its patents. While proceedings concerning EP 2 375 749 and EP 2 774 375 are pending at Munich Regional Court, Nokia filed an action regarding EP 2 661 892 at the UPC local division Munich. All relate to the H.265 video codec standard.

In early January, Hisense took a licence while the lawsuits against Acer and Asus continue.

Taiwanese computer manufacturer Acer responded with its own lawsuits against Nokia concerning wireless communication technology at Munich Regional Court. In a parallel UK action, the implementers asked the High Court to determine a RAND rate for Nokia’s video streaming portfolio. Just before Christmas, UK High Court judge James Mellor granted an interim licence to Acer and Asus (case IDs: HP-2025-000030, 000032 ).
items.ai_summary_zh: 慕尼黑地方法院裁定华硕与宏碁侵犯诺基亚视频编码专利并下达禁令，认定被告不愿获得许可。此案与英国高等法院授予临时许可形成对比，标志着慕尼黑法院在 SEP 纠纷中进一步细化 FRAND 抗辩标准，强化了专利权人地位。

items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["德国法院倾向保护专利权人，企业须主动展示许可意愿", "跨国诉讼需统筹英德裁判差异，优化全球许可谈判布局"]

items.title: Federal Court of Justice hears FRAND appeal in VoiceAge vs HMD
items.title_zh: 德联邦最高法院审理 VoiceAge 诉 HMD 案中的 FRAND 上诉
items.summary: Today, the German Federal Court of Justice hears FRAND aspects in the appeal in VoiceAge vs HMD and will scrutinise Munich's FRAND approach.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Carpmaels & Ransford Simmons + Simmons LLP BARDEHLE PAGENBERG View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Alex Wilson Dr. Sabine Dethof Nigel Stoate View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Today, the German Federal Court of Justice scrutinises Munich Higher Regional Court's new FRAND approach. The highest court's decision in the VoiceAge vs HMD case will have far-reaching consequences for European FRAND law. Once again, the EU Commission has intervened in the proceedings. JUVE Patent reporter Mathieu Klos is at the hearing.

After five years, FRAND is once again on the agenda in Karlsruhe. The city is home to the Federal Court of Justice, Germany’s highest civil court and final instance in patent proceedings. In 2020 and 2021, the court set out its interpretation of the CJEU’s European FRAND case law in Huawei vs ZTE with its two judgments Sisvel vs Haier I and II.

Today, the court will hear its next FRAND case in the appeal in VoiceAge vs HMD over EVS speech-coding patent EP 2 102 619 (case ID: KZR 10/25).

Previously, the Higher Regional Court Munich under presiding judge Lars Meinhardt found that HMD infringed VoiceAge’s EP 619 and is not a willing licensee (case ID: 6 U 3824/22 Kart).

The Regional Court Munich had also found HMD to be an unwilling licensee and ordered HMD to cease and desist (case ID: 7 O 14091/19 ).

HMD not only appealed to the Higher Regional Court Munich but also to the Federal Court of Justice in 2025. Given the fundamental importance of the FRAND-related issues, the Higher Regional Court Munich granted HMD leave to appeal to the Federal Court of Justice, but only concerning FRAND.
items.ai_summary_zh: 德国联邦最高法院正在审理 VoiceAge 诉 HMD 案的 FRAND 上诉，重点审查慕尼黑高等地区法院的新 FRAND 方法。此前下级法院认定 HMD 侵权且非善意被许可人。欧盟委员会再次介入诉讼。作为德国最高民事法院，其判决将基于华为诉中兴案确立的原则，对欧洲 FRAND 法律产生深远影响。
items.ai_core_points_zh: ["德国最高院审查慕尼黑法院新 FRAND 判定方法", "欧盟委员会介入案件，凸显其对竞争政策的重要性", "判决将重新定义欧洲标准必要专利善意许可标准"]
items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧涉及 SEP 许可时，需密切关注德国最高院对“善意被许可人”的最新界定。建议提前评估合规风险，优化谈判策略，避免因被认定为非善意而面临禁令，同时利用欧盟委员会介入机会争取有利政策环境。
items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["关注德国最高院对善意许可标准的最新司法界定", "优化 SEP 谈判策略以规避禁令风险并应对监管"]

items.title: Munich Regional Court further clarifies FRAND rules in Wilus vs Asus judgment
items.title_zh: 慕尼黑法院在 Wilus 诉华硕案中进一步澄清 FRAND 规则
items.summary: A recent judgment in Asus vs Wilus by Munich Regional Court is peppered with guiding principles for the Chamber's FRAND jurisprudence.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Thum&Partner Carpmaels & Ransford VOSSIUS & PARTNER Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte mbB View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Varuni Paranavitane, PhD Thomas Schachl Franca Poll-Wolbeck View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

VOSSIUS & PARTNER Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte mbB

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

A partial payment obligation, comparative agreements for FRAND licences and FRAND calculation. A recent judgment by Munich Regional Court's 7th Civil Chamber is peppered with guiding principles for the chamber's FRAND jurisprudence. The judges ordered Asus to cease and desist due to the infringement of a Wilus wifi patent. In future, the chamber will not only look at the implementer's willingness to take a licence but also at the FRAND conformity of the SEP holder's offer.

Implementer Asus, SEP holder Wilus and their lawyers received a judgment yesterday that packs a punch. Munich Regional Court ordered Asus to cease and desist from infringing the Wifi 6 patent EP 3 512 289 . This much had been known since mid-January. Ever since, Asus has not been allowed to sell certain Wifi-6-compatible devices in Germany (case ID: Ref. 7 O 5007/25).

These include the Zenfone 11 Ultra smartphone, the ASUS Zenbook S 13, the ASUS Chromebox 4 mini PC and the ASUS AiO E3 (E3402) all-in-one PC. However, the plaintiff, the South Korean Wilus Institute of Standards and Technology, has not yet enforced the judgment. Asus can also appeal, which is considered certain.

The background to the lawsuit is the conclusion of a licence agreement for Sisvel’s Wifi 6 portfolio or bilaterally with the owner of the patent Wilus and SK Telecom. Last year, the South Korean R&D lab also launched a separate action against Asus at the Mannheim UPC local division alleging infringement of a second Wifi 6 patent.

Yesterday, the opposing parties and their lawyers received the written judgment with many fundamental comments, some of which went beyond the case. The 7th Civil Chamber has not had the opportunity to solidify its FRAND guideline for some time as many recent SEP cases have settled early. What the judges have now written into the Wilus vs Asus judgment is groundbreaking for future SEP proceedings at Munich Regional Court.
items.ai_summary_zh: 慕尼黑地方法院第七民事庭就 Wilus 诉华硕案作出判决，裁定华硕侵犯 WiFi 6 标准必要专利并责令停止侵权。该案不仅确认了禁令救济，更关键的是确立了新的 FRAND 审查原则：未来法院将同时评估实施者的许可意愿与权利人的要约是否符合 FRAND 条件，为后续 SEP 诉讼提供重要指引。

items.title: LexisNexis launches new tool for cleaner SEP data
items.title_zh: LexisNexis 推出新工具净化 5G 标准必要专利数据
items.summary: LexisNexis launches tool to clean data for 5G patents to aid in licensing negotiations and FRAND-rate determination.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles HOYNG ROKH MONEGIER DHS Thum&Partner View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Markus Adamczyk Lutz Kietzmann Dr. Eva Ehlich View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

The US information and data service company LexisNexis has launched a new tool to clean up data for 5G patents. The tool should aid companies for issues such as licensing negotiations and FRAND-rate determination. However, it is unlikely to solve important problems such as the over declaration of SEPs or the essentiality check.

LexisNexis Legal & Professional has launched the ‘Cellular Verified’ initiative. According to the company , through a year-long collaboration with major SEP holders in the telecoms sector, LexisNexis was able to achieve 99.9% data accuracy by matching, cleaning, enhancing, and verifying its database of patent families with the data in the ETSI database.

The European Telecommunications Standards Institute is the world’s most important institute for the declaration of standard essential patents. Patent holders can introduce their patents into a standard via the institute. The problem, however, is that ETSI does not check whether the patents are really relevant to the standard. It also does not determine the patents’ economic value, which often leads to lawsuits if the companies cannot agree on a rate for a licence.

The initiative also addresses inconsistent data. “Patent declaration data is inherently inconsistent, shaped by each company’s unique declaration practices,” says analyst Tim Pohlmann from LexisNexis Intellectual Property Solutions.

“If such self-declarations are not rigorously cleaned and verified, the data favours companies that provide more detailed and cleaner information, resulting in a systematic bias,” Pohlmann adds.

Many ETSI-declaring companies now self-declare patents at the earliest possible stage, frequently including unpublished provisional or priority numbers. This process introduces significant variability, as companies follow different approaches when declaring their patents. For example, companies often submit patent numbers in varying formats and types, states LexisNexis in a press release.

items.title: UK Court of Appeal denies Tesla a pool licence
items.title_zh: 英国上诉法院驳回特斯拉获取专利池许可的诉求
items.summary: UK Court of Appeal denies Tesla pool licence in the car manufacturer's dispute with Avanci and its licensor InterDigital.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Finnegan, Henderson, Farabow, Garrett & Dunner, LLP Grünecker GOTTSCHALD View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Tom Oliver Dr. Jan Gottschald Elisabeth Greiner View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

Finnegan, Henderson, Farabow, Garrett & Dunner, LLP

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

The UK courts have once again denied Tesla a 5G pool licence. The ruling was handed down today by the UK Court of Appeal in the car manufacturer's dispute with Avanci and its licensor InterDigital. But the judgment was not clear-cut.

The Court of Appeal panel comprising presiding judge Richard Arnold, Philippa Whipple and Stephen Phillips heard the appeal at the beginning of December. While Whipple and Phillips have now dismissed Tesla’s appeal, Arnold allowed it in part. Arnold believes the UK patent court has the authority to rule on a global FRAND rate for a pool licence.

As a consequence, the Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal in its entirety ( case ID: CA-2024-001749 ). According to the ruling, Tesla originally complained that Avanci has only offered a flat rate of $32 per vehicle for such a licence on a non-negotiable basis. Tesla contend that this rate far exceeds a FRAND rate.

Tesla had always confirmed that it needed a 5G licence in the UK. The UK is the fourth largest market for Tesla globally. Consequently, Tesla has undertaken to accept a FRAND licence on the terms determined by the patent court. Avanci and InterDigital have disputed the jurisdiction of the UK courts for determining a pool licence and have now prevailed with this position.

The Court of Appeal judges thus confirmed the first-instance ruling.
items.ai_summary_zh: 英国上诉法院在特斯拉与 Avanci 及 InterDigital 的纠纷中，再次驳回特斯拉获取 5G 专利池许可的请求。尽管主审法官 Arnold 认为英国法院有权裁定全球 FRAND 费率，但合议庭最终以多数意见维持原判，确认英国法院对此类专利池许可缺乏管辖权。特斯拉曾承诺接受法院确定的 FRAND 条款，但权利方成功抗辩了管辖权异议。
items.ai_core_points_zh: ["英国上诉法院维持原判，驳回特斯拉关于确定全球专利池费率的诉求", "合议庭多数意见认定英国法院无权对 Avanci 专利池许可行使管辖权", "特斯拉虽承诺接受 FRAND 裁决，但未能推翻权利方对管辖权的成功抗辩"]
items.ai_insight_zh: 中国车企出口欧洲需警惕单一法域诉讼风险，英国法院管辖权受限可能迫使企业在多国应诉。建议提前评估专利池许可策略，避免陷入被动，并关注不同法域对全球费率裁定的态度差异。
items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["英国管辖权受限，中国车企需防范多国并行诉讼带来的合规成本上升", "应主动规划全球 FRAND 许可策略，避免因单一市场争议波及整体业务布局"]

items.title: ETSI prepares for EU SEP regulation by appointing a chief policy officer
items.title_zh: ETSI 任命首席政策官以应对欧盟 SEP 监管改革
items.summary: Martin Chatel is newly appointed as chief policy officer at ETSI.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte Partnerschaft mbB Maikowski & Ninnemann TALIENS View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Markus Kreuzberg K. Victoria Barker, Ph.D. Dr. Antje Brambrink View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte Partnerschaft mbB

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Martin Chatel has been newly appointed chief policy officer at ETSI.

Martin Chatel has been appointed chief policy officer at standards body ETSI. He took up the role on 10 February, which entails leading the policy affairs department. In its announcement yesterday, the standards body said Chatel “will spearhead ETSI’s strategic policy engagement with the European Commission” and other bodies, associations, policymakers and stakeholders, reporting to ETSI Director-General Jan Ellsberger. Chatel holds a masters in international law from Sciences Po Paris and an LLM from the Institute of European Studies.

He has previously held a variety of roles including senior IP legal services and product manager (patents) at IP firm Dennemeyer & Associate in Germany and manager of legal affairs at Cenelec (the European Electrotechnical Committee for Standardization) in Brussels. Ellsberger says, “The Policy Affairs Department will play a crucial role in supporting the European Union’s commitment to ethical, secure, and privacy-preserving technology deployment.” Chatel’s responsibilities are likely to include representing the interests of ETSI in the context of the EU Commission’s planned SEP reform, which will affect a large proportion of the patents in the ETSI standards.

One of the key proposals is a competence centre, which would carry out non-binding essentiality checks. Additionally, FRAND-rate determination by mediators would be mandatory, if non-binding, before parties can then litigate in UPC countries.

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validity Test When EPO Opposition Is Pending 17 February 2026 by Simmons + Simmons LLP
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧洲电信标准协会（ETSI）任命 Martin Chatel 为新任首席政策官，旨在加强与欧盟委员会的战略互动。Chatel 将领导政策事务部，重点应对欧盟拟议的标准必要专利（SEP）改革，包括建立能力中心进行必要性检查及强制调解 FRAND 费率。此举标志着 ETSI 正积极调整架构，以适应即将落地的欧盟 SEP 监管框架及统一专利法院（UPC）的诉讼环境。

items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧持有大量通信 SEP，需密切关注欧盟新规对许可谈判流程的重塑。建议提前评估专利组合必要性，适应强制调解机制，并关注 ETSI 与欧盟政策协同带来的合规成本变化，优化在欧知识产权战略布局。

items.title: CMS strengthens team with two UPC and SEP litigators from Klaka and Freshfields
items.title_zh: CMS 吸纳两名 UPC 及 SEP 诉讼律师以强化团队
items.summary: CMS Hasche Sigle has recruited two new partners, Stephan Dorn from Freshfields and Constantin Kurtz from Klaka.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles KATHER AUGENSTEIN NLO | European Patent & Trademark Attorneys EIP View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Dr. Andrew Lin Denis Monégier du Sorbier Prof. Dr. Heinz Goddar View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

NLO | European Patent & Trademark Attorneys

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Patent partner moves are currently in vogue, and the CMS network is no exception. Its German law firm CMS Hasche Sigle has recruited two new partners. Stephan Dorn joins from Freshfields and Constantin Kurtz from Klaka. These hires significantly expand CMS' European patent litigation team with a view to the UPC.

Despite established teams in Germany and the UK, CMS’ pan-European patent practice has so far lacked visibility in UPC litigation. CMS does not act as a merged practice, but through the respective patent teams of its network law firms, such as CMS Hasche Sigle. These cooperate closely in patent law, but neither the German nor UK practices have yet appeared before the new court. This is now set to change.

Stephan Dorn will join as partner in the Düsseldorf office on 1 July. The 46-year-old was previously counsel in Freshfields’ patent team. He began his career as a trainee lawyer at Hogan Lovells in 2012 before becoming an associate in the patent team.

In summer 2017, Dorn joined the Freshfields patent team led by Frank-Erich Hufnagel. Dorn has focused on SEP and FRAND litigation since the beginning of his career.

Dorn was part of the Freshfields team that advised Continental and Lenovo in lawsuits filed by Nokia over mobile phone patents. With the launch of the UPC, he became increasingly prominent.
items.ai_summary_zh: 德国 CMS Hasche Sigle 律所从 Freshfields 和 Klaka 引进两位合伙人，旨在提升其在统一专利法院（UPC）的诉讼能力。此前该所虽在德英有基础，但尚未涉足 UPC 诉讼。新加入的 Stephan Dorn 专长于标准必要专利及 FRAND 纠纷，曾参与诺基亚相关大案，此次扩充标志着 CMS 正式布局泛欧专利诉讼市场。

items.title: InterDigital and Bird & Bird challenge UK High Court jurisdiction in Amazon case
items.title_zh: InterDigital 挑战英国高院在亚马逊案中的管辖权
items.summary: Today the UK High Court judge Richard Meade hears InterDigital's jurisdictional challenge in the RAND-rate setting dispute.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Wildanger DHS PAUSTIAN & PARTNER View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Sven Krause, LL.M. Ari Laakkonen Dr. Friedrich Emmerling View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

There is much at stake for Amazon today as UK High Court judge Richard Meade hears InterDigital's jurisdictional challenge in the RAND-rate setting dispute. If Meade denies his court's jurisdiction, it would deal a blow to the US internet giant.

The dispute between InterDigital and Amazon over a licence to InterDigital’s video streaming portfolio is still in its early stages, but the two opponents have already met several times in court. So far, the main issues have centred on anti-interim-licence injunctions and anti-suit injunctions at the UK High Court and the UPC’s Mannheim local division. The dispute has already attracted significant international attention.

However, the courts have yet to address the core issues: Is Amazon entitled to a FRAND rate set by the UK High Court? Is InterDigital entitled to injunctions in the UPC territory and Germany?

Today and tomorrow, the parties will again face off at the UK High Court. Judge Richard Meade will hear arguments on whether the court has jurisdiction to set a global FRAND rate for InterDigital’s portfolio, as Amazon has requested.

In late August 2025, Amazon sought a declaration of non-infringement at the High Court, alleging InterDigital had abused a dominant position. Amazon also sought declarations concerning InterDigital’s obligations under the ITU-T FRAND commitment (case ID: HP-2025-000043).

The US-based SEP owner is challenging the London court’s jurisdiction. Barristers Michael Bloch from Blackstone Chambers and Tom Jones from 8 New Square are representing InterDigital today in the Rolls Building courtroom on Fetter Lane.
items.ai_summary_zh: 英国高院法官 Richard Meade 审理 InterDigital 提出的管辖权异议，争议焦点在于法院是否有权设定全球 FRAND 费率。该案同时涉及德国曼海姆地方法院与统一专利法院（UPC）的禁令冲突。若英国法院被裁定无管辖权，将对亚马逊造成重大打击，目前双方正就核心法律问题进行激烈辩论。
items.ai_core_points_zh: ["InterDigital 质疑英国高院设定全球 FRAND 费率的管辖权", "案件核心涵盖英国费率裁决权与德/UPC 地域禁令的冲突", "亚马逊主张 InterDigital 滥用支配地位并寻求不侵权宣告"]
items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧遭遇 SEP 诉讼时，需警惕多国平行诉讼风险。应提前评估英国全球费率裁决与 UPC 禁令的联动效应，制定统筹英、德及 UPC 的抗辩策略，避免陷入被动局面。
items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["关注英国管辖权动向以预判全球费率裁决风险", "统筹应对 UPC 禁令与成员国诉讼的交叉影响"]

items.title: US firms secure Asian implementers interim licence against Nokia in the UK
items.title_zh: 美律所助亚洲企业在英获诺基亚临时许可
items.summary: Asus, Acer and Hisense have won the right to an interim licence following a UK High Court ruling last Thursday.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles HOYNG ROKH MONEGIER Grünecker Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte Partnerschaft mbB View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Victoria A. Randall, Ph.D. Penny Gilbert Dr. Gisbert Hohagen View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte Partnerschaft mbB

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Acer, Asus and Hisense have won interim licence dispute in the UK following a High Court ruling last Thursday. While Kirkland & Ellis represents Hisense, the decision marks the first major victory for Morgan Lewis and its clients Acer and Asus since the US law firm established its London patent litigation team.

The UK High Court has seen numerous FRAND and RAND rate determination and interim licence disputes recently, including Samsung versus ZTE, Warner Bros. and Paramount versus Nokia, and Amazon versus InterDigital.

Last Thursday, the UK High Court under presiding judge James Mellor granted an interim licence to Acer, Asus and Hisense (case IDs: HP-2025-000030, 000032, 000039 ).

The three Asian implementers obtained a declaration that a willing licensor in Nokia’s position would agree to an interim licence until the court determines “reasonable and non-discriminatory” terms for a patent licence.

The companies had requested that the UK High Court determine a RAND rate for Nokia’s video streaming portfolio. The case concerns Nokia’s portfolio of standard-essential patents (SEPs) declared essential to the H.264/AVC and H.265/HEVC video coding standards administered by the ITU-T. The standard is subject to Swiss law.
items.ai_summary_zh: 英国高等法院裁定华硕、宏碁和海信有权获得针对诺基亚的临时许可。法官 James Mellor 宣布，若许可方愿意，应在法院确定最终 FRAND 费率前提供临时许可。此案涉及 H.264/HEVC 视频编码标准必要专利，标志着美方律所在伦敦团队的首次重大胜利，反映了英国在 SEP 费率裁决中的核心地位。

items.title: RAND licence battle in InterDigital against Amazon continues over Christmas
items.title_zh: InterDigital 诉亚马逊 RAND 许可战圣诞期间持续升级
items.summary: The UPC and Regional Court Munich confirm their ex parte AILI orders. The UK High Court maintains its jurisdiction to rule on a RAND rate determination in Amazon against InterDigital.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Finnegan, Henderson, Farabow, Garrett & Dunner, LLP TALIENS PAUSTIAN & PARTNER View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Elisabeth Greiner Benoît Strowel Dr. Moritz Meckel View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

Finnegan, Henderson, Farabow, Garrett & Dunner, LLP

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

The UPC and the Regional Court of Munich have confirmed their ex parte AILI orders, while at the same time the UK High Court maintains its jurisdiction to rule on a RAND rate determination on InterDigital's video streaming portfolio. These decisions have pleased Amazon and the SEP holder just before Christmas. However, these decisions, issued just before christmas, meant that both the implemntor and the SEP holder had positive news to take with them into the festive season.

The Unified Patent Court never sleeps. This analysis had already prevailed in 2024 and also applies to the Christmas season this year. Shortly before the holidays, the Mannheim local division under presiding judge Peter Tochtermann published its decision in Amazon’s appeal against an ex parte anti-interference licence injunction (AILI).

InterDigital had received this from both the local division and the Regional Court of Munich in a dispute over a licence for its video streaming portfolio at the end of September. At that time, both rulings from Mannheim and Munich prevented Amazon from seeking an interim licence in the UK that would have extended to UPC and continental European jurisdictions. Both courts held that compulsory interim licences undermine the fundamental right of patent enforcement.

On 22 December, the Mannheim local division not only rejected Amazon’s objection to the ex parte order and thus also confirmed the AILI. At the same time, the court also ordered an unusually high penalty if Amazon does not comply with the order.

The judgement of the local division states: “In case any or all of the Respondents disobey this Order, a penalty of up to € 50.000.000 may be set.”
items.ai_summary_zh: 统一专利法院（UPC）曼海姆分院与慕尼黑地区法院确认针对亚马逊的单方反干扰许可禁令（AILI），并设定高额违令罚金。与此同时，英国高等法院坚持其对 InterDigital 视频流媒体组合进行 RAND 费率裁定的管辖权。双方当事人在节前分别获得有利裁决，凸显欧英两地并行诉讼的复杂格局。

items.title: Avanci enters market for licensing wi-fi SEPs
items.title_zh: Avanci 进军 Wi-Fi 标准必要专利许可市场
items.summary: Avanci has expanded beyond cellular connectivity into wi-fi technology and launched a new platform for wi-fi 6 and wi-fi 7 SEPs.
items.content:
Firms & Experts Sponsored: Firm Profiles Thum&Partner Maikowski & Ninnemann BARDEHLE PAGENBERG View all Firm Profiles Sponsored: Expert Profiles Heinrich Georg Pannen Alexander Ortlieb Ingo Bauer, Ph.D. (WHUT) View all Expert Profiles Sponsored Content UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP View all Sponsored Content

UPC Provisional Measures: Dual Validi… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Lessons from dapagliflozin in the UK:… by Pinsent Masons LLP

The Unified Patent Court and Damages:… by Simmons + Simmons LLP

Patent pool Avanci has now expanded beyond cellular connectivity into wi-fi technology. It has launched a new platform covering wi-fi 6 and, in future, wi-fi 7 standard essential patents. German luxury carmaker Mercedes-Benz has signed on as the first licensee.

Avanci now joins the likes of patent pool Sisvel in licensing wi-fi technology. Today, Avanci announced the launch of its new wi-fi licensing platform aimed specifically at the automotive industry. Its first programme under the new platform, Avanci Wi-Fi 6 Vehicle, consolidates essential wi-fi 6 patent rights into a single licence for automotive manufacturers.

The new platform will also cover SEPs for wi-fi 7.

The Avanci Wi-Fi 6 Vehicle programme brings together ten patent owners. These are Atlas Global, BlackBerry, Canon, ISG, KPN, Malike Innovations, Meizu, NEC, NTT, and Oppo. Avanci states that the licence covers thousands of patented technologies essential to implementing wi-fi 6 connectivity in vehicles.

Avanci has not publicly disclosed the royalty rate per-vehicle, but says the figure is available to prospective licensees on a confidential basis. An early licensee discount applies for companies that conclude a licence agreement before their first sale of a wi-fi-6-compliant vehicle, or within six months of the programme’s launch.

Mercedes-Benz is the first car manufacturer to have signed a patent sublicence agreement under the programme. Formerly known as Daimler, the carmaker and Avanci pool member Nokia were previously involved in a fierce dispute in German courts over mobile communication SEPs in the so-called connected car wars.
items.ai_summary_zh: 专利池运营商 Avanci 宣布将其业务从蜂窝通信扩展至 Wi-Fi 技术领域，推出涵盖 Wi-Fi 6 及未来 Wi-Fi 7 标准必要专利的新许可平台，首期聚焦汽车行业。梅赛德斯 - 奔驰成为首位被许可人。该平台整合了包括诺基亚、OPPO 等十家权利人的专利，旨在为车企提供一站式许可方案，费率保密但设有早期签约折扣。

items.title: Standard essential patents (SEPs): a new board for the global geostrategic game
items.title_zh: 标准必要专利：全球地缘战略博弈的新棋盘
items.summary: Standard essential patents (SEPs) are patents that protect technologies that are indispensable for implementing a technical standard.
items.content:
If a manufacturer wants to comply with the standard —for example 4G, 5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, video codecs or V2X in automobiles— it cannot “design around” those patents without failing to comply. Essentiality arises within standardisation bodies (SSOs/SDOs) —ETSI, IEEE, 3GPP, ITU, ISO, among others— where companies contribute technology to define common specifications that ensure global interoperability. In exchange for having their technology incorporated into the standard, proprietors usually commit to license their SEPs under certain conditions that we call FRAND, which stands for Fair, Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory. It is a commitment that the SEP proprietor makes to the SSO and, in practice, to the market. The balance FRAND seeks to strike is a delicate one: avoiding the proprietor’s hold-up (blocking markets with an SEP) without encouraging the implementer’s hold-out (using the technology without paying, hoping to litigate later). SEPs are, therefore, a structural building block of the digital economy: they enable global markets for interoperable devices, but concentrate technological power in portfolios that can condition entire supply chains. This debate has traditionally been an issue for the telecoms sector with its 2G/3G/4G/5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, NFC standards, etc., but today it is a critical issue for all sectors with ”connected things”: Consumer electronics, automotive, industry, energy, etc. In particular, the expansion into automobiles and industry explains the increase in disputes and regulatory moves in recent months. Situation in the US, Asia and the EU In the United States, at the end of 2025, the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) announced the creation of the Standard-Essential Patent (SEP) Working Group, a cross-functional team reporting directly to the Director of the USPTO. The group is co-chaired by senior legal officials from the Office and was created with a clear narrative: to strengthen an ecosystem that, according to the USPTO itself, had become ‘hostile’ to innovators due to unclear rules and downward pressure on licence fees. As a first tangible initiative, in January 2026 the USPTO has launched the Standards Participation and Representation Kudos (SPARK) pilot programme, which grants a limited number of acceleration certificates (to speed up the review of applications or appeals) to entities that participate significantly in standardisation. In Japan, in June 2025, the Tokyo District Court issued what is considered to be the first SEP-based injunction in Japan in Pantech v. Google, ordering the cessation of sales of the Pixel 7. In this case, the Court distinguishes between two phases: a negotiation phase (where methodological disagreements are tolerated), and a conciliation phase supervised by the court, with methodology and information requirements. The turning point has been the lack of cooperation from the implementer in the supervised phase (including the refusal to provide sales data needed to verify proposals). Japan ”opens the door” to the application of injunctive relief in the case of SEP infringement, but with a demanding standard that is closely linked to procedural conduct. In turn, China has become a central forum for SEP disputes because of its willingness to set global royalties and its use of procedural tools such as anti-suit injunctions (ASIs). This has generated friction with other jurisdictions and has led to an international trade dimension: the EU took China to the WTO over practices related to setting global royalties, and the TRIPS compatibility debate has become part of the geopolitical chessboard of SEPs. In 2023, the European Commission presented an ambitious proposal for a Regulation on SEPs and FRAND (transparency, registration, verification of essentiality and specific procedures) promoted by the European automotive industry. Following strong opposition from industry and Member States, the proposal was withdrawn. The political reading is two-fold: on the one hand, the need for more transparency and predictability in the SEP market is recognised. On the other hand, there is caution about the creation of mandatory procedures that could make licensing more expensive and slower, affecting European competitiveness. The result is a period of rethinking. It is plausible that the EU will return with a more modular reform (registration, reporting and best practices) that is less interventionist in economic negotiations. At the same time, the Commission is using trade (WTO) to address the extraterritorial effects of SEP litigation. The challenge of the next decade will be to preserve the incentive to contribute technology to standards —the engine of the digital economy— without allowing blocking power or strategic procrastination to distort entire markets. A large part of global technological competitiveness is at stake in this balance, and Europe must make its move in the coming months. The difficulty lies in managing the interests of all parties, both technology providers and users. Once again, patents are at the heart of the strategy. Written by: Luis Ignacio Vicente del Olmo . Strategic Advisor at PONS IP.

Standard essential patents (SEPs) are patents that protect technologies that are indispensable for implementing a technical standard. If a manufacturer wants to comply with the standard —for example 4G, 5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, video codecs or V2X in automobiles— it cannot “design around” those patents without failing to comply.

Essentiality arises within standardisation bodies (SSOs/SDOs) —ETSI, IEEE, 3GPP, ITU, ISO, among others— where companies contribute technology to define common specifications that ensure global interoperability. In exchange for having their technology incorporated into the standard, proprietors usually commit to license their SEPs under certain conditions that we call FRAND, which stands for Fair, Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory. It is a commitment that the SEP proprietor makes to the S

items.title: JUB – Compétence confirmée dans l’affaire Sun Patent Trust c/ Vivo
items.title_zh: 统一专利法院巴黎分院确认 Sun 诉 Vivo 案管辖权
items.summary: JUB : la division locale de Paris a rendu une ordonnance rejetant l’exception d’incompétence soulevée par Vivo dans le litige l’opposant à Sun Patent Trust.
items.content:
Le 30 octobre 2025, la division locale de Paris de la Juridiction unifiée du brevet (JUB) a rendu une ordonnance (UPC_CFI_361/2025) rejetant l’exception d’incompétence soulevée par Vivo dans le litige l’opposant à Sun Patent Trust.

L’affaire concerne le brevet européen EP 3 852 468, relatif aux technologies 4G+, dont Sun Patent Trust reproche la contrefaçon par la commercialisation de divers modèles de smartphones Vivo en Europe.

Vivo soutenait que la JUB n’était pas compétente pour statuer sur la détermination des conditions FRAND, présentées par le demandeur comme partie intégrante de son action, et contestait également la compétence territoriale de la division parisienne.

Sun Patent Trust arguait au contraire qu’il s’agissait d’une action en contrefaçon de brevet essentiel (SEP), relevant de l’article 32(1)(a) UPCA, et que la question des conditions FRAND n’était pas une demande autonome mais une condition préalable à l’octroi d’une injonction.

Les magistrats de la division locale ont considéré que l’action principale était bien une action en contrefaçon et que les questions FRAND pouvaient être examinées à titre incident, conformément à la jurisprudence de la JUB et à l’arrêt Huawei v. ZTE. L’admissibilité de la sous-demande relative aux conditions FRAND sera tranchée au fond.

Sur le plan territorial, la Cour a jugé que l’achat d’un produit Vivo sur le site Fnac.com, destiné au marché français, suffisait à établir la compétence de la division locale de Paris au sens de l’article 33(1)(a) UPCA.

Cette ordonnance confirme la compétence de la JUB pour traiter les litiges SEP/FRAND dans le cadre d’actions en contrefaçon et illustre une approche pragmatique de la compétence territoriale, fondée sur des actes de commercialisation en ligne. L’affaire se poursuivra désormais sur le fond, avec un débat attendu sur la conformité des offres de licence aux exigences FRAND et sur l’octroi éventuel d’une injonction contre Vivo.

items.title: FCJ: Requirements for Licensee Willingness under FRAND
items.title_zh: 德联邦法院：FRAND 抗辩中被许可人意愿的认定要求
items.summary: The German Federal Court of Justice clarifies the requirements for willingness to license in FRAND defence cases.
items.content:
Recent case law from the Munich patent courts has further refined the requirements for raising a competition law-based FRAND defence against injunctive relief based on standard-essential patents. Several decisions of the Regional Court of Munich I in 2025 and 2026 address in particular the assessment of an implementer’s willingness to take a license, the role of partial payments and security, and the determination of a permissible FRAND licensing range on the basis of comparable license agreements.

In its judgment of 8 January 2026 (Regional Court of Munich I, case no. 7 O 5007/25), the court emphasized that the assessment of willingness to license depends not only on formal declarations but also on the implementer’s concrete conduct in negotiations. In particular, the court considered it highly relevant whether the implementer pays at least an undisputed portion of the licence fee or provides adequate security, as such conduct may demonstrate that the implementer is not engaging in hold-out behaviour.

In another judgment of 5 February 2026 (Regional Court of Munich I, case no. 7 O 7655/25), the same chamber further elaborated on its approach to determining a FRAND royalty corridor based on comparable licence agreements. According to the court, comparable agreements may serve as a benchmark for identifying a permissible range of licence rates within which an offer will generally be considered FRAND-compliant.

Similarly, in its decision of 22 January 2026 (Regional Court of Munich I, case no. 7 O 4102/25) concerning patents allegedly essential to the H.265/HEVC video coding standard, the court rejected a competition law compulsory licence defence and held that the defendant had not demonstrated conduct sufficient to establish a successful FRAND defence.

Earlier, the Regional Court of Munich I had also addressed the international dimension of SEP disputes. In its judgment of 26 November 2025 (Regional Court of Munich I, case no. 21 O 12112/25), the court held that the competition law assessment of FRAND obligations with respect to German patents must, in principle, be carried out by German courts. It therefore considered attempts to obtain a court-ordered “interim licence” from a foreign court affecting German patents to be incompatible with the territorial nature of patent rights.

Taken together, these decisions illustrate how German first-instance courts are increasingly translating the framework established by the Court of Justice of the European Union in Huawei v ZTE into concrete procedural and economic criteria. Against this background, the recent judgment of the German Federal Court of Justice is of particular significance.

On 27 January 2026 (KZR 10/25), the Bundesgerichtshof dismissed HMD Global Oy’s appeal in the dispute with VoiceAge EVS GmbH. The judgment confirms the decisions of the Munich lower courts and further refines the standards governing FRAND defences against injunctive relief based on standard-essential patents.

The legal framework remains Article 102 TFEU and the principles developed by the Court of Justice of the European Union in Huawei v ZTE.

VoiceAge asserted claims based on a European patent declared essential to the EVS telecommunications standard. HMD argued that enforcement of the injunction would constitute an abuse of dominance, as it had been willing to take a licence on FRAND terms.

Both the Regional Court of Munich I and the Higher Regional Court of Munich rejected this defence, finding that HMD had not demonstrated sufficiently consistent willingness. The appeal raised, in particular, the question whether the Huawei framework must be applied in a strict chronological sequence and how security is to be assessed.
items.ai_summary_zh: 德国联邦法院在 HMD Global 诉 VoiceAge 案中驳回上诉，确认慕尼黑下级法院判决。裁判细化了标准必要专利（SEP）禁令救济中 FRAND 抗辩的适用标准，强调被许可人意愿不仅看声明，更需考察具体谈判行为、部分付款或担保提供情况，并明确德国法院对涉及德国专利的 FRAND 义务拥有管辖权。

brief.headline_zh: 欧盟执法升级与地理标志新规下的应对
brief.headline_en: EU Enforcement Surge and New GI Rules
brief.summary_zh: 本期 EUIPO 动态显示欧盟正全面升级知识产权执法，联合 Europol 打击假药及有组织犯罪，并推广 IPEP 数字工具提升扣货效率。同时，手工艺地理标志新体系正式落地，外观设计检索库扩容。司法层面明确纯功能形状不可注册商标，但认可“单点实体 + 线上”模式构成商标真实使用。
key_points: 欧盟联合多机构升级执法，利用 IPEP 数字工具强化海关对假冒商品的拦截能力。
手工艺与工业地理标志（CIGIs）咨询委员会成立，双阶段审查程序即将实施。
司法判例明确：纯功能性形状拒注，但本地服务结合网络可证成欧盟商标使用。
actions: 利用 DesignView 新版图像搜索功能，在产品设计阶段强化在先权利排查。
评估中国传统工艺品申请欧盟地理标志保护的可行性，提前布局国家初审策略。
针对在欧业务，采用“单点实体 + 电商平台”模式低成本维持欧盟商标有效性。
watchlist: 关注 EUIPO 公益法律支持项目，微型企业可申请免费维权援助。
监控欧盟打击有组织犯罪（EMPACT）行动，防范供应链合规风险。
追踪地理标志双阶段审查的具体国别名单及异议应对细则。

items.title: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines
items.title_zh: EUIPO 支持全欧打击假药重大行动
items.summary: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines
items.content: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines EUR 32 million worth of fake medicines and illicit doping substances have been seized across Europe in Operation SHIELD VI, a major operation led by Europol with the support of the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO), alongside other international partners. Carried out between April and November 2025, the operation brought together authorities from 30 European countries. It led to nearly 500 arrests, more than 3,300 prosecutions and investigations into over 40 organised crime groups. In addition, 66 websites used to sell counterfeit medicines were shut down. The seized products included over 3 million fake pills and nearly 300 000 illegal packages of doping substances, including Semaglutide-based products marketed as fat-burners. Some of these counterfeit medicines closely resemble legitimate products, including opioids, which significantly increases the risk of poisoning and overdose. Through its support to Operation SHIELD VI, the EUIPO contributed its expertise in tackling intellectual property crime and analysing online infringement trends linked to counterfeit pharmaceuticals, reinforcing coordinated action to protect consumers and legitimate businesses across the EU. A growing threat for European consumers According to the latest data, trade in counterfeit and illicit medicines is on the rise, coming at a high cost not only for individual consumers but also for society as a whole. Beyond infringing intellectual property rights, these products pose a serious threat to public health, as they often contain dangerous, substandard or unknown substances. The misuse of prescription and over-the-counter medicines, together with the growth of unregulated websites selling fake medicines, continues to fuel illegal markets and further complicates enforcement efforts by making detection and interception more difficult. To better protect consumers from counterfeit medicines and promote safer online purchases, the EUIPO, together with the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and Europol, launched the campaign “Stay on top – EU vs Fake Medicines”. The initiative raises awareness about the risks of buying medicines from unverified online sources and provides guidance on how to identify authorised sellers.

items.title: IPEP Presented in Athens: Strengthening IP Enforcement Through Digital Tools
items.title_zh: EUIPO 于雅典展示 IPEP：以数字工具强化知识产权保护
items.summary: IPEP Presented in Athens: Strengthening IP Enforcement Through Digital Tools
items.content: IPEP Presented in Athens: Strengthening IP Enforcement Through Digital Tools The EUIPO´s IP Enforcement Portal (IPEP) was presented during the 1st edition of the ‘training for officers on the field’ co‑organised by the Greek Independent Authority for Market Control and Consumer’s Protection and the Global Anti-Counterfeiting Group (GACG), represented by INDICAM. The event was supported by the EUIPO, and took place last week, 27 February 2026 in Athens, Greece. It gathered representatives of all Greek competent authorities that detect and seize counterfeit such as customs, municipal police and the authority for market control. The training addressed officers working on the field, being on the front line to protect the Greek market from counterfeit items. It brought together right holders and national authorities to exchange practical information on the detection of fakes. About the event The one-day hands-on training was co‑organised by the Greek Independent Authority for Market Control and Consumer’s Protection and the Global Anti-Counterfeiting Group, represented by INDICAM, with support from the EUIPO. The training aimed to support frontline law enforcement officers in targeting and detecting counterfeit goods. It provided practical information on frequently counterfeited products such as automobile spare parts, fashion apparel, and tobacco products presented by the right holders themselves. Around 15 companies presented their IP portfolios, key infringement challenges, and technical guidance for identifying counterfeit items. IPEP: A unified digital tool for enforcement authorities At the event, the EUIPO team showcased how IPEP supports Greek and EU enforcement officers by enabling secure, real‑time information exchange between authorities and rights holders. IPEP Mobile App The team also presented the mobile version of IPEP, offering field‑ready access to: Multilingual, real‑time search to IP rights, product and contact point information on the spot. suspicious‑case (detention notification) creation and syncing— all optimised for rapid use by Greek enforcement officers. Moving Forward The seminar reaffirmed the commitment of the Greek national authorities, industry stakeholders represented by the GACG and the EUIPO to deepen cooperation and expand the use of digital enforcement tools like IPEP across Greece. Additional training sessions and onboarding support will continue throughout 2026–2027.
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局（EUIPO）在希腊雅典联合当地市场监管机构及全球反假冒组织，举办针对一线执法人员的培训活动。会议重点展示了知识产权执法门户（IPEP）及其移动应用，旨在通过数字化手段实现权利人与执法部门间的实时信息交换，提升对汽车零件、时尚及烟草等假冒商品的查获效率，并计划于 2026 至 2027 年深化在希腊的合作与推广。

items.ai_insight_zh: 中国出口企业应关注欧盟数字化执法趋势，主动利用 IPEP 平台备案知识产权。建议企业配合当地执法部门提供真伪鉴别技术支持，建立快速响应机制，以降低产品在希腊及欧盟市场的被侵权风险。

items.title: EUIPO holds first Plenary Meeting of the Advisory Board for Craft and Industrial Geographical Indications
items.title_zh: EUIPO 召开手工艺与工业地理标志咨询委员会首次全会
items.summary: EUIPO holds first Plenary Meeting of the Advisory Board for Craft and Industrial Geographical Indications
items.content: EUIPO holds first Plenary Meeting of the Advisory Board for Craft and Industrial Geographical Indications Today, the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) held the first plenary meeting of the Advisory Board for Craft and Industrial Geographical Indications (CIGIs) marking an important milestone in the implementation of the new EU framework for the protection of craft and industrial geographical indications. The meeting provided an initial platform for exchange on key horizontal and organisational issues linked to the implementation of the CIGI system aiming at ensuring an efficient, coherent and consistent application of the new Regulation across the European Union. Supporting EUIPO’s new competence The Advisory Board plays a central role in supporting EUIPO’s newly acquired competence in the field of craft and industrial geographical indications. Its mandate is threefold: to provide general opinions on the assessment of quality criteria, the establishment of a geographical indication’s reputation, the determination of the generic nature of names and the assessment of potential risks of consumer confusion. to offer expert opinions on specific geographical indication applications when requested by EUIPO’s Geographical Indications Division, the Boards of Appeal or the European Commission. and to act as a forum for cooperation between Member States, the Commission and the Office, facilitating the exchange of best practices and promoting the efficiency of national procedures. Key topics and outcomes During its first plenary meeting the Advisory Board elected its Chairperson and Deputy Chairperson, formally enabling the body to begin operating under the Regulation and its Rules of Procedure. Also, members discussed the mandate and working methods of the Advisory Board, received updates on the implementation of the Craft and Industrial Geographical Indications Regulation from EUIPO and the European Commission and exchanged views on progress at national level in the Member States. The meeting also included an initial discussion on the Guidelines for Geographical Indications for craft and industrial products, which will support consistent application of the new system. Next steps Following this inaugural meeting, the Advisory Board will become fully operational and ready to exercise its functions in line with the Geographical Indications Regulation and its Rules of Procedure. EUIPO’s Geographical Indications Division, and where applicable the Boards of Appeal, may consult the Advisory Board at any stage of the procedures for registration, opposition, appeal, amendment of product specifications or cancellation of registrations. The establishment and activation of the Advisory Board represent a significant step forward in the roll-out of the EU system for the protection of craft and industrial geographical indications, reinforcing cooperation among EU institutions and Member States and contributing to the effective protection of Europe’s cultural and industrial heritage.
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局（EUIPO）召开手工艺与工业地理标志（CIGIs）咨询委员会首次全会，标志欧盟该领域保护框架正式实施。会议选举了领导层，明确了委员会在质量评估、声誉认定及防止名称通用化方面的咨询职能，并讨论了实施指南。此举旨在确保新条例在全欧范围内高效、一致地适用，加强成员国与欧盟机构间的协作。

items.title: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO
items.title_zh: 强化打击有组织犯罪：EMPACT 传播者网络会议在欧盟知识产权局召开
items.summary: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO
items.content: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO The European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) hosted the European Multidisciplinary Platform Against Criminal Threats (EMPACT) Communicators Network in Alicante, bringing together communication experts from across the European Union to strengthen how joint action against serious and organised crime is explained to the public. Participants highlighted the importance of effective communication, as EMPACT enters a new operational cycle (2026–2029). Among the objectives: sustain political momentum, support operational excellence, reinforce public confidence and raise awareness of EU action against serious and organised international crime. Discussions focused as well on the need of strengthening the visibility of EMPACT, as a cornerstone of EU law enforcement cooperation. Cooperation at all levels EMPACT is the EU’s flagship framework for cooperation against serious and organised crime. It brings together EU Member States, EU agencies and international partners to identify common priorities and deliver coordinated investigations and operational actions targeting the most significant criminal threats facing Europe. Through EMPACT, law enforcement authorities across Europe work together to dismantle criminal networks involved in areas such as trafficking, fraud, cybercrime and intellectual property crime. These joint efforts translate EU priorities into concrete operational results on the ground. Protecting citizens, businesses and the internal market. Making this impact visible is essential. Clear and strategic communication helps to show how EU cooperation works, strengthens trust among institutions and citizens, and ensures that operational achievements are understood beyond specialist audiences. The EMPACT Communicators Network plays a key role in aligning messages, sharing best practices and amplifying results across Europe. The European Union Intellectual Property Office is hosting this year’s EMPACT Communicators Network meeting to help strengthen cooperation among network members.
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局在阿利坎特主办 EMPACT 传播者网络会议，汇聚欧盟各国沟通专家，旨在加强针对严重有组织犯罪的联合行动宣传。会议聚焦提升 EMPACT 作为欧盟执法合作基石的可见度，涵盖打击贩运、欺诈及知识产权犯罪等领域，以巩固公众信心并支持 2026-2029 新运营周期。

items.title: Intellectual Property for all: The critical importance of IP rights to SMEs
items.title_zh: 知识产权惠及全员：IP 权利对中小企业的至关重要性
items.summary: Intellectual Property for all: The critical importance of IP rights to SMEs
items.content: Intellectual Property for all: The critical importance of IP rights to SMEs By Eleonora Rosati, Professor of IP Law, Stockholm University, LinkedIn Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) represent 99.8% of all businesses in the European Union (EU) and intellectual property (IP) is central to their commercial success and also to the overall competitiveness of the EU. Whether you are launching a new product, opening a fashion boutique featuring local brands, or building a tech start-up, IP rights (IPRs) can make a difference as a strategic asset which can help you unlock new opportunities and safeguard the value of your ingenuity. Protecting intangible assets – including for example your name, logo, products, services, and creative outputs – is essential to positioning your business for long-term success. Why we need to talk about the importance of IP for SMEs The strategic importance of IP for the EU´s economy is undeniable. As it is outlined in a recent joint study of the European Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) and the European Patent Office (EPO), companies that hold IP rights perform better than those that do not: they generate 23.8% more revenue per employee than companies without IPRs. When adjusting for factors like industry, company size, and location, this revenue advantage increases to 41%, with an even greater impact observed among SMEs. When a SME registers its IPRs, it achieves in general 44% higher revenue per employee compared to their counterparts without such protection. When we speak about SMEs focused on innovation – including tech startups – another joint EUIPO/EPO study indicates that IPR‑intensive industries attract the overwhelming majority of private equity and venture capital investment in the EU. Over 88% of total funding, equivalent to €70.7 billion, was invested between 2021 and 2023 in startups operating in IPR‑intensive industries, underscoring the importance of IP in fostering innovation and growth. Furthermore, it is reported that IPR-intensive industries pay significantly higher wages than other industries, with a wage premium of 40.9%. These numbers are in line with those reported in the European Commission´s recently published 2025 Annual Single Market and Competitiveness Report. While also showcasing the critical role of IP to commercial success and as a signal of firms’ innovativeness, the report indicates that startups are ten times more likely to obtain early-stage venture capital if they have registered patents or trade marks. Also, IPRs are associated with a 200% higher likelihood of successful exit. It is thus clear that IP plays a key role, and so is obtaining the relevant protections under IP law. But have you ever thought of what role different IPRs play in helping your SME or startup thrive? Different IPRs to protect different intangible assets Starting with your name and logo, these can be protected as trade marks. If you, for example, register a trade mark with the EUIPO, you will receive protection for the entire EU territory. A key requirement is that your name or logo is sufficiently distinctive, by that meaning that the sign in question will be perceived by consumers of the relevant goods and/or services as a badge of commercial origin. Furthermore, if you operate a website, the look-and-feel of your website and its domain name will be also protected by IP. The appearance of your products may be safeguarded through design rights or copyright. Registered designs offer broader and longer-lasting protection than unregistered ones, which arise automatically. If, for example, you want to register your design and receive protection across the entire EU territory, once again you will need to apply to the EUIPO. Copyright also arises automatically and requires originality, that is: the result of free and creative choices. This means that, if your product’s appearance is dictated purely by technical considerations, copyright protection will not apply. If your product or service includes an invention – for example, a novel technology in a hair dryer or an innovative fabric in your garments – you may seek patent protection. And do not forget that confidential business information – such as methods, recipes, or formulas – can be protected as trade secrets. How the EU and the EUIPO support SMEs with their IP Considering the numbers and the role that different IPRs play, it is clear that getting the IP ‘right’ should be a priority for any company, not only for SMEs. It is for all these reasons that the Ideas Powered for business SME Fund has been devised by the European Commission and implemented by the EUIPO. This is a grant scheme designed to help EU-based SMEs protect their IPRs. The new edition was launched on 2 February and will run until 4 December 2026. SMEs can also receive advice in their preferred language from experts across Europe on how to protect their IPRs, as well as what to do in case someone claims that their own rights are being infringed. Of course, all of this is also consistent with the broader long-term strategy and priorities of the EUIPO, including awareness raising, promotion, and enforcement of IPRs. If one for example reviews the EUIPO’s 2030 Strategic Plan, Goal 3 is all about improving access to the IP system and promote the use of IPRs in support of innovation-driven economic growth. This can be also achieved by helping the next generation of innovators to thrive, creating innovative products and services, promoting innovation, value creation and inclusion. In sum: If you want your business or start-up to thrive tomorrow, start thinking about your IP strategy today!

items.title: New EU System for Geographical Indications for Craft and Industrial Products – important reminder for applicants
items.title_zh: 欧盟手工业产品地理标志新体系申请重要提醒
items.summary: New EU System for Geographical Indications for Craft and Industrial Products – important reminder for applicants
items.content: New EU System for Geographical Indications for Craft and Industrial Products – important reminder for applicants As of 1 December 2025, the Office has assumed competence for registering applications for the new intellectual property right covering Geographical Indications for Craft and Industrial Products (CIGIs). By 2 December 2026, all existing national protection systems will be phased out and replaced by the new EU system. Under the standard procedure, EU producer groups and other applicants from twenty EU Member States can submit applications for the protection of new CIGIs to their respective national competent authorities. These authorities will conduct the national phase examination, which includes examining the application and launching the national opposition procedure. Once the national examination phase is successfully completed, the competent authorities will forward the application to the Office for the examination at Union phase to take place. This phase involves verifying the absence of manifest errors, initiating the EU-level opposition procedure, and ultimately registering the CIGI in the Union register. Applicants from remaining seven EU Member States - from Denmark, Finland, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, the Netherlands and Sweden will file applications directly with the EUIPO. In addition, EU Member States may seek protection for any existing CIGIs protected under national law or established by use prior to 1 December 2025. To facilitate the process, the Office has developed GIportal, an intuitive e-filing tool for CIGIs. The first weeks of implementation have already demonstrated strong interest in the scheme. In fact, until 27 February 2026, the Office published 64 applications for existing names submitted by France, Portugal, Slovakia and Sweden primarily in the sectors of stones and minerals, textiles, and ceramics. Webinars, workshops, detailed FAQs as well as countless other sources of useful information can be found on the EUIPO’s GI Hub.
items.ai_summary_zh: 自 2025 年 12 月 1 日起，EUIPO 正式负责注册手工业和工业产品地理标志（CIGIs）。至 2026 年 12 月，各国原有制度将全面被欧盟新体系取代。多数成员国申请人需经国家阶段审查后转交 EUIPO，而丹麦等七国可直接向 EUIPO 申请。目前已有多国提交现有名称保护申请，EUIPO 推出 GIportal 工具并配套指南以协助过渡。

items.title: Pro Bono Legal Support for SMEs: Call for Design Rights Experts as demand grows
items.title_zh: EUIPO 呼吁外观设计专家加入 SMEs 公益法律支持项目
items.summary: Pro Bono Legal Support for SMEs: Call for Design Rights Experts as demand grows
items.content: Pro Bono Legal Support for SMEs: Call for Design Rights Experts as demand grows The EUIPO Pro Bono Programme continues to expand, confirming the importance of early-stage IP support for European SMEs, particularly microenterprises, without professional representation. In 2025, the programme received 1,375 SME support requests, the highest number since its launch and part of a steady annual increase since 2020. Over 90% of applicants were microenterprises, and requests came from all EU Member States, with strongest participation from Germany, Spain, Italy, France and the Netherlands, alongside growing engagement from smaller countries. The EUIPO extends its sincere thanks to all the IP practitioners who give generously of their time to provide the pro bono service. What SMEs request Demand remains strongest for trade mark-related support, but requests also cover design protection, disputes and documentation: 49% of SMEs needed help preparing an IP application 23% sought guidance on selecting the most appropriate IP rights SMEs report very high satisfaction, with 9 out of 10 recommending the programme, citing the speed, quality and professionalism of the expert support. IP Experts: A Growing Network The Pro Bono network now includes 289 active IP experts, with 165 new members in 2025, and strong representation in Italy, Germany and Spain. Support spans all major IP fields, including trade marks and designs, with most consultations lasting 1- 4hours, ensuring sustainability for practitioners. Experts are largely satisfied with their participation, and some pro bono interactions have evolved into long-term professional relationships. Focus for 2026: Design Protection In 2026, the programme will place a stronger emphasis on engaging designers and design-driven SMEs, reinforcing the need for IP representatives with expertise in design rights. Outreach will be expanded, the expert network further strengthened, and user experience improved for both SMEs and professionals. IP practitioners with experience in design protection are particularly encouraged to join the EUIPO Pro Bono Expert Network and support Europe’s creative and innovative businesses at a critical stage of their development. To work as a Pro Bono Expert, sign up here

items.title: All in a name: Why ‘George Orwell’ cannot be registered as a trademark
items.title_zh: 全在名字里：为何“乔治·奥威尔”无法注册为商标
items.summary: Following the EUIPO’s confirmation that the famous author’s name cannot be trademarked for certain goods and services, Paula Vener of Inventa examines how this decision was reached.
items.content: Following the EUIPO’s confirmation that the famous author’s name cannot be trademarked for certain goods and services, Paula Vener of Inventa examines how this decision was reached. Registering the name of a famous author as a trademark raises a familiar clash. There is a legitimate commercial interest in licensing a name and stopping third parties from marketing ‘official’ goods that are not, in fact, authorised. At the same time, EU trademark law is built around a narrower function. It protects signs that consumers use to identify commercial origin, and it refuses registration where the sign will be understood mainly as information about the goods or services, such as their content or subject matter. Case R2248/2019‑G puts that tension in sharp focus and asks a direct question: in the markets at issue, does ‘GEORGE ORWELL’ operate as a trademark, or is it simply a cultural reference that describes what the product is about? The estate of Sonia Brownell Orwell applied to register the word sign ‘GEORGE ORWELL’ as a European Union trademark (EUTM) for a wide range of goods and services, in particular in Classes 9, 16, 28 and 41.
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局确认著名作家“乔治·奥威尔”之名不得在特定商品服务上注册。文章分析指出，尽管权利人希望防止未授权商品，但欧盟商标法核心在于标识来源。若公众将该名称视为对作品内容或主题的描述性文化参考，而非商业来源标识，则依据相关法律规定必须拒绝注册。
items.ai_core_points_zh: ["名人姓名注册需区分商业来源标识与文化内容描述功能", "若名称被理解为商品信息而非品牌，欧盟法律将拒绝注册", "奥威尔遗产方申请在多类商品注册遭拒引发法律界限讨论"]
items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧申请含名人或文化符号商标时，须警惕描述性驳回风险。建议提前评估名称在目标市场的认知属性，避免将公共文化资源误作私有品牌，必要时调整策略或补充显著性证据以应对审查。
items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["评估文化符号在欧认知属性，规避描述性驳回风险", "勿将公共资源误作私有品牌，需提前布局显著性证据"]

items.ai_summary_zh: 在 Altendorfer 诉 EUIPO 案中，欧盟普通法院确认，位于德国小城市的单一牛排馆，若结合线上展示及国际声誉，可构成指定欧盟的酒店餐饮类国际商标的真实使用。该判决澄清了欧盟商标法下，局部地域使用配合数字存在即可满足维持商标效力的要求，无需在全欧盟范围内广泛铺开实体业务。

The General Court's judgment in Empreinte v EUIPO (T-437/25) offers a cautionary tale for designers hoping to protect innovative but technically driven product shapes.   Background In March 2024 the French company Empreinte filed an application for registration of the
items.ai_summary_zh: 法国公司 Empreinte 申请注册手形握把开瓶器为欧盟商标被拒。欧盟普通法院在 T-437/25 号案件中维持原判，认定该形状主要由技术功能决定，缺乏显著性且具功能性特征。此案警示设计师，纯技术驱动的产品形状难以获得商标保护，即便其具有创新性。

items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧申请产品形状商标时，应避免纯粹功能性设计。建议结合非功能美学元素提升显著性，或转而寻求外观设计专利保护，以规避因功能性被驳回的风险。

items.title: The never-ending story of Brexit – The Final Chapter
items.title_zh: 欧盟法院裁定：商标异议中在先权利须存续至决定作出时
items.summary: The Court of Justice of the EU (“CJEU”) has rendered its long-awaited decision in EUIPO v Nowhere (C-337/22), disagreeing with the Advocate General’s (“AG”) opinion. In short, the CJEU held that, in opposition proceedings, the earlier right must continue to exist until the time the European Intellectual Property Office (“EUIPO”) or its Board of Appeal (“BoA”) renders the decision.   Background In
items.content: The Court of Justice of the EU (“CJEU”) has rendered its long-awaited decision in EUIPO v Nowhere (C-337/22), disagreeing with the Advocate General’s (“AG”) opinion. In short, the CJEU held that, in opposition proceedings, the earlier right must continue to exist until the time the European Intellectual Property Office (“EUIPO”) or its Board of Appeal (“BoA”) renders the decision.   Background In
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟法院在 EUIPO v Nowhere 案中作出终审判决，推翻总检察长意见。法院明确，在商标异议程序中，作为异议基础的在先权利必须持续有效，直至欧盟知识产权局或其上诉委员会作出最终决定之时。该裁决厘清了脱欧背景下英国商标在欧盟异议程序中的效力截止点，确立了以“决定作出时”为判断基准的关键规则，对后续类似跨境权利冲突案件具有指导意义。
items.ai_core_points_zh: ["欧盟法院裁定在先权利须存续至异议决定作出之时", "判决推翻总检察长意见，确立新的程序性审查标准", "明确脱欧后英国商标在欧盟异议程序中的效力界限"]
items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业在欧进行商标布局或应对异议时，需动态监控英国等第三国在先权利状态。若依赖此类权利提出异议，必须确保其在欧盟官方作出决定前依然有效，避免因权利中途失效导致异议失败，建议提前制定备选维权方案。
items.ai_insight_points_zh: ["依赖第三国权利异议需确保持续有效至决定作出", "应建立权利状态动态监控机制以防中途失效风险"]

brief.headline_zh: 欧英升级打假执法，中企需防刑事风险
brief.headline_en: EU and UK escalate anti-counterfeit enforcement
brief.summary_zh: 2026 年初，欧盟与英国显著升级知识产权刑事执法。EUIPO 联合 Europol 打击假药及有组织犯罪，UKIPO 获长期资金扩充专项组。执法重点转向供应链合规与 AI 数据源审查。在欧中企面临更高刑事追责风险，亟需从被动应对转向主动合规，利用新机制维权并规避卷入犯罪网络。
key_points: 欧盟强化跨机构协作，EUIPO 联手 Europol/Eurojust 重点打击假药及有组织 IP 犯罪。
英国确认延长 IP 犯罪专项组资金至 2029 年，并启动公私合作试点提升执法效率。
版权执法延伸至 AI 领域，出版商起诉影子图书馆，训练数据来源合规成焦点。
actions: 立即开展供应链侵权隐患排查，建立针对假药及高危产品的专项合规审查机制。
主动对接英国 IP 犯罪专项组新试点，利用官方渠道提升维权响应速度与精准度。
严格审核 AI 研发数据源，禁止使用未授权影子图书馆资源，防范版权连带诉讼。
watchlist: 欧盟 EMPACT 网络后续发布的涉华有组织犯罪预警名单及具体案例。
英国新行业合作试点的具体准入标准及中企参与维权的实际操作流程。
五大出版商诉影子图书馆案的判决走向及其对 AI 训练数据合法性的界定。

items.title: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines
items.title_zh: EUIPO 支持全欧打击假药重大行动
items.summary: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines
items.content: EUIPO supports major Europe-wide action against counterfeit medicines EUR 32 million worth of fake medicines and illicit doping substances have been seized across Europe in Operation SHIELD VI, a major operation led by Europol with the support of the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO), alongside other international partners. Carried out between April and November 2025, the operation brought together authorities from 30 European countries. It led to nearly 500 arrests, more than 3,300 prosecutions and investigations into over 40 organised crime groups. In addition, 66 websites used to sell counterfeit medicines were shut down. The seized products included over 3 million fake pills and nearly 300 000 illegal packages of doping substances, including Semaglutide-based products marketed as fat-burners. Some of these counterfeit medicines closely resemble legitimate products, including opioids, which significantly increases the risk of poisoning and overdose. Through its support to Operation SHIELD VI, the EUIPO contributed its expertise in tackling intellectual property crime and analysing online infringement trends linked to counterfeit pharmaceuticals, reinforcing coordinated action to protect consumers and legitimate businesses across the EU. A growing threat for European consumers According to the latest data, trade in counterfeit and illicit medicines is on the rise, coming at a high cost not only for individual consumers but also for society as a whole. Beyond infringing intellectual property rights, these products pose a serious threat to public health, as they often contain dangerous, substandard or unknown substances. The misuse of prescription and over-the-counter medicines, together with the growth of unregulated websites selling fake medicines, continues to fuel illegal markets and further complicates enforcement efforts by making detection and interception more difficult. To better protect consumers from counterfeit medicines and promote safer online purchases, the EUIPO, together with the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and Europol, launched the campaign “Stay on top – EU vs Fake Medicines”. The initiative raises awareness about the risks of buying medicines from unverified online sources and provides guidance on how to identify authorised sellers.

items.title: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO
items.title_zh: 强化打击有组织犯罪：EMPACT 传播者网络会议在欧盟知识产权局召开
items.summary: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO
items.content: Amplifying the fight against organised crime: EMPACT Communicators Network at EUIPO The European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) hosted the European Multidisciplinary Platform Against Criminal Threats (EMPACT) Communicators Network in Alicante, bringing together communication experts from across the European Union to strengthen how joint action against serious and organised crime is explained to the public. Participants highlighted the importance of effective communication, as EMPACT enters a new operational cycle (2026–2029). Among the objectives: sustain political momentum, support operational excellence, reinforce public confidence and raise awareness of EU action against serious and organised international crime. Discussions focused as well on the need of strengthening the visibility of EMPACT, as a cornerstone of EU law enforcement cooperation. Cooperation at all levels EMPACT is the EU’s flagship framework for cooperation against serious and organised crime. It brings together EU Member States, EU agencies and international partners to identify common priorities and deliver coordinated investigations and operational actions targeting the most significant criminal threats facing Europe. Through EMPACT, law enforcement authorities across Europe work together to dismantle criminal networks involved in areas such as trafficking, fraud, cybercrime and intellectual property crime. These joint efforts translate EU priorities into concrete operational results on the ground. Protecting citizens, businesses and the internal market. Making this impact visible is essential. Clear and strategic communication helps to show how EU cooperation works, strengthens trust among institutions and citizens, and ensures that operational achievements are understood beyond specialist audiences. The EMPACT Communicators Network plays a key role in aligning messages, sharing best practices and amplifying results across Europe. The European Union Intellectual Property Office is hosting this year’s EMPACT Communicators Network meeting to help strengthen cooperation among network members.
items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局在阿利坎特主办 EMPACT 传播者网络会议，汇聚欧盟各国沟通专家，旨在加强针对严重有组织犯罪的联合行动宣传。会议聚焦提升 EMPACT 作为欧盟执法合作基石的可见度，涵盖打击贩运、欺诈及知识产权犯罪等领域，以巩固公众信心并支持 2026-2029 新运营周期。

items.ai_summary_zh: 随着不安全假冒商品风险上升，英国政府宣布将资助伦敦-based 知识产权犯罪专项组至 2029 年。此举旨在加强执法能力，并与欧盟知识产权局及欧洲当局日益严格的反假冒行动保持协同，共同打击跨境侵权活动，保障市场安全。

news_items.summary: Once a year in October, European patent judges meet in Venice for the European Judges’ Forum. news_items.ai_summary_zh: 文章指出每年十月举行的威尼斯欧洲法官论坛是专利法官闭门交流思想的重要平台，律师通常被排除在外。鉴于当前欧洲专利诉讼环境变化，评论强调亟需召开一次临时会议，以确保法官间能继续就关键程序与裁判标准进行非公开的深度沟通，维护司法一致性。
news_items.ai_insight_zh: 中国企业应关注欧洲专利法官内部协调动态，这可能预示 UPC 裁判标准的微调。建议密切跟踪会议后续成果，评估对在建案件的影响，并提前调整诉讼策略以应对潜在的司法倾向变化。

news_items.content: L’Office de l’Union européenne pour la propriété intellectuelle (EUIPO) est l’agence de l’Union européenne (UE) chargée de la gestion des marques de l’Union européenne, des dessins et modèles enregistrés de l’Union européenne, des indications géographiques pour les produits artisanaux et industriels, ainsi que de la coopération européenne et internationale dans le domaine de la (suite) L’article L’EUIPO enregistre le plus grand nombre de dépôts de son histoire est apparu en premier sur Propriété intellectuelle - Paris .
news_items.ai_summary_zh: 欧盟知识产权局（EUIPO）作为负责管理欧盟商标、外观设计及地理标志的机构，近期记录了其成立以来的最高申请量。该数据反映了欧洲市场对知识产权保护的强劲需求，凸显了 EUIPO 在协调欧洲及国际 IP 合作中的核心地位，标志着欧盟知识产权活动达到新的里程碑。
news_items.ai_insight_zh: 申请量激增意味着审查周期可能延长或竞争加剧。中国企业应提前布局欧盟商标与外观，避免抢注风险；同时需密切关注 EUIPO 程序变化，优化申请策略以应对日益拥挤的注册环境。
